title: Lab: Repair a Heat-Safety Event Flyer
summary: Lab: Repair a Heat-Safety Event Flyer retrieves prior skills in an original unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply css absolute units, css font relative units, css percentage units, css viewport dynamic units, css math fluid sizing in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

options.text: Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 1: use centimeters for all screen text because physical measurements are always more consistent.
Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 1: choose px for suitable screen-relative details and physical units for controlled print constraints, then verify zoom and actual output.
Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 1: avoid every absolute unit because a one-pixel border can never zoom.

options.text: Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 3: an em value always refers to the root font size.

Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 3: name the reference for each property, calculate nested results, and test root font changes plus long content before choosing rem, em, ch, or lh.
Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 3: use ch as an exact physical character count for every font and language.

options.text: Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 5: every percentage resolves against viewport width.
Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 5: identify the property, containing block, writing mode, and definite dimensions, then calculate and measure the percentage result.
Unfamiliar print and mobile event flyer sizing incident case 5: replace every percentage with viewport width because the viewport is always the containing block.
